title: How Long Does it Really Take to Get an E-2 Visa?
content:
## E-2 Visa Processing Time: How Long It Really Takes and Smart Ways to Speed It Up
- E-2 Processing Times
- Your Personalized E-2 Timeline
- Why Attorney Guidance is Key

## E-2 Visa Processing Times
When planning an investment in the United States under the E-2 Treaty Investor Visa , one of the most common questions for investors is: “How long will it take?” Processing time is an essential consideration for business plans, travel schedules, and family transitions.  Processing times can vary widely depending on whether you file through a U.S. consulate abroad or with U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) inside the U.S., and whether you add the Premium Processing option.
Developing the right strategy begins with understanding what influences timing. This overview explains what to expect in 2025 and how our team helps investors navigate the process efficiently, from filing strategy to interview preparation, to achieve a faster and smoother E-2 approval.
Consular Filing Abroad
USCIS Change/Extension of Status in U.S.
File at a U.S. consulate in your country of citizenship or residence
File directly with USCIS from inside the U.S.
Available for E-2 filings with USCIS
Total Time Components
Document preparation + consular review + interview/issuance + passport return
Document preparation + USCIS review (regular or premium)
2 weeks to 4 months* (depending on consulate/post)
Regular: over 13 months*; Premium Processing (Form 1907): USCIS takes 15 business days to issue a decision (if no RFE) with an additional $2,965 fee
Consulate appointment availability (should check the post’s current wait times before planning), Administrative Processing
USCIS processing timelines and possibility of Requests for Evidence (RFEs)
*Processing times show how long past cases took, not how long yours will take. Every case is different, and actual timing depends on workload and case details.

### Why Do E-2 Processing Times Vary So Much?
The E-2 Treaty Investor Visa allows nationals of treaty countries to invest a substantial amount of capital in a bona fide U.S. enterprise. Petitioners can pursue this status either through consular processing, which involves applying for a visa at a U.S. consulate abroad, or through USCIS, by filing a change or extension of status from within the United States using Form I-129.
Key Reasons Processing Timelines Vary :
- Different decision makers: Consular officers and USCIS adjudicators follow different procedures and timelines.
- Local post rules: Every consulate has its own E-2 intake and review workflow.
- Appointment supply schedule: Visa interview slots fluctuate by season and staffing.
- Case quality: Strong, well-organized filings are less likely to trigger RFEs or administrative processing.
Important note for fall 2025: U.S. consulates now require in-person interviews for virtually all E-2 applicants. Interview waiver eligibility has been narrowed significantly and currently applies only to diplomatic visa categories and certain B-1/B-2 renewals. E-2 applicants should plan a personal interview and check their consulate’s current scheduling policies early. Read More about the E2 Visa Extension Requirements

## Your Personalized E-2 Timeline: A Quick Calculator
Every E-2 case follows the same general stages, but the total timeline depends on the applicant’s readiness, the chosen filing route, and government processing capacity.
Step 1: The process begins with document preparation, which typically takes two to six weeks depending on how quickly the necessary evidence can be assembled. This includes the business plan, proof of lawful investment funds, corporate formation records, and either the DS-160 or I-129 form. Well-prepared and organized documentation is the best safeguard against future delays.
Step 2: Once the materials are gathered, the case enters the government’s queue. Petitioners filing through a U.S. consulate should plan for a mandatory in-person interview. Appointment availability varies significantly by post and wait times can fluctuate throughout the year. The Department of State publishes current estimated interview wait times on its Visa Appointment Wait Times page, which is worth checking early and monitoring regularly. Some consulates also have pre-screening or document submission requirements that differ by location. Because E-2 timelines are not always listed publicly, verifying requirements directly with the consulate or through your attorney remains the most reliable approach. Those filing with USCIS should review current I-129 processing times using the USCIS Processing Times too l. Premium Processing guarantees that USCIS will take adjudicative action within 15 business days, though it does not shorten preparation time and does not guarantee approval.
Step 3: The decision phase follows. Consular applicants attend an interview, and in some cases, the application may undergo brief administrative processing before issuance. USCIS applicants can expect either approval, a Request for Evidence (RFE), or a Notice of Intent to Deny (NOID) within the published timeframe, or within the fifteen-day window if Premium Processing applies.
Step 4: Once approval is issued, the case concludes with visa issuance or status activation. Consular applicants typically receive their visa within a few days to two weeks following the interview, while USCIS petitioners receive online confirmation immediately, followed by mailed approval notices. For consular cases, E-2 status becomes effective upon admission to the United States. For USCIS filings, the change of status becomes active as of the approval date.
Pro tip: Thorough preparation, accurate documentation, and early coordination with counsel remain the most effective ways to streamline this process and avoid unnecessary delays.

## Choosing your E-2 Path

## Why Attorney Guidance is Key to Timely E-2 Approvals in 2025
E-2 visa processing continues to vary significantly, not only between consulates and USCIS, but among individual consulates themselves. Because nationality, residence, and local procedure often dictate the filing path, professional guidance is essential to achieving the most efficient outcome.
At Colombo & Hurd, we approach every E-2 case with careful strategy and precision. Our team prepares audit-ready filings that meet the standards of both consular and USCIS adjudicators. We account for post-specific requirements such as pre-screen submissions, email-only filings, and page or document-length limits. Each filing is meticulously organized with clear labeling and well-supported exhibits, including detailed business plans that demonstrate financial projections, staffing plans, and contractual evidence of operations.
We also work with clients to identify the most strategic filing route, outlining the timing considerations and procedural differences between consular and USCIS submissions. This approach helps minimize the risk of Requests for Evidence while maintaining steady momentum toward approval.
Even within consistent legal frameworks, each E-2 case is unique. By tailoring every step to the applicant’s specific circumstances and maintaining clear communication throughout the process, we help ensure a timely and effective path to E-2 approval.

## Ready to Start Your E-2 Journey?
Processing times can feel unpredictable. But with the right preparation, strategy, and guidance, you can minimize delays and move forward with confidence. Every day you wait is a day your U.S. business plans are on hold.
At Colombo & Hurd, we guide entrepreneurs and investors through every step of the E-2 process. With over two decades of experience and more than 10,000 approvals, our team is familiar with the requirements of both consulates and USCIS and works to position each case as effectively as possible.
